top.title: PAAN Co., Ltd. — Opening the path to dishes yet undiscovered
top.description: Corporate site of PAAN Co., Ltd. We build MENU-PAAN, a multilingual menu translation service.
top.hero_title: Opening the path to dishes yet undiscovered.
top.hero_sub:
The dishes we wish to taste, the drinks we long for, the aromas we wish to feel, the tables we wish to touch.
The walls of language and culture between them, we shall surmount with technology and artificial intelligence.
top.news_2026_06_03_release: PAAN Co., Ltd. will be founded.
top.news_2026_06_03_product: We shall launch our multilingual menu translation service MENU-PAAN.
top.news_2026_05_10_info: Our corporate website is now live.

mission.title: Mission | PAAN Co., Ltd.
mission.description: Opening the path to dishes yet undiscovered. Restaurants have crossed borders, but dishes have not. Through technology and AI, we bridge cultures through food.
mission.page_title: Opening the path to dishes yet undiscovered.
mission.question_h: Restaurants have crossed borders. But dishes?
mission.question_p1: In 1900, Michelin published its guide. In 1979, Zagat began its journey from New York. Tabelog, OpenTable, Google Maps. — Information about restaurants, over the course of a century, has indeed crossed borders. We can now compare star ratings and reviews on the palms of our hands — in Paris, in New York, in Shibuya.
mission.question_p2: And yet, a thought arises. Restaurants have crossed borders. But have the dishes themselves crossed?
mission.question_p3: Sit at a restaurant in Paris, and if you cannot read the menu, you will only order what you already know. Open the dim sum menu in Hong Kong with over a hundred items, and you will still not know what suits your taste. Wish to open a Château Lafite from your wedding year, and even with the bottle in mind, there are nights when it remains beyond reach. — "You won't know unless you go," "You won't know unless you order" — these walls remain quietly intact, even after the information revolution.
mission.question_p4: Are we truly meeting the dishes we want to eat, the wines we want to drink? — And could we not bring them together?
mission.regrets_h: The dining table holds many regrets.
mission.story1_p: You summon courage and step into a restaurant from the guidebook. Murmuring a hesitant Bonjour, you signal the number of guests with your fingers. The interior takes your breath away. You are seated. You open the menu. — You cannot read it. You search each item on Google, only to find dry, literal translations. In the end, you order ratatouille and onion soup, dishes you already know. Or perhaps you take a chance on something unknown, and a plate utterly unlike what you imagined arrives. You glance at your neighbour's dish and realise — "That. That is what I wanted."
mission.story2_lead: The moment you open a dim sum menu in Hong Kong.
mission.story2_p: Names of dim sum stretch on and on. Thirty, fifty, over a hundred dishes. Tracing the characters does not help you tell them apart. You cannot tell how much each portion is. How many should one person order? What is the right balance between steamed and fried? — Someone, anyone, suggest something. "For your taste, perhaps these three to start." That single phrase — it is nowhere to be found in the sea of search results.
mission.story3_lead: That wine. That sake. Once more.
mission.story3_p: You wish to open a Château Lafite from the year of your wedding, with your wife. You long to taste again that fine sake from Fukui you encountered on a business trip, in that very tokkuri. — The drink we crave is, more often than not, already chosen in our minds. And yet, we have no method beyond chance to reach the establishment that serves it. You ring a celebrated French restaurant only to hear, "We have one from a year before, perhaps..." An hour passes as you scan menus, restaurant by restaurant. Some nights, even when you know exactly what you want to drink, you cannot find it.
mission.story4_p: You wish to drink the local sake. Kagatobi. Tokikigen. Kuroobi. But not merely to drink — to savour each one alongside the local dish that pairs with it best. "Noto sea bream with Kagatobi." "Cod milt with Tokikigen." — We have no way, save chance, to find the establishment that offers such pairings.
mission.story5_lead: Through the days of dietary restriction.
mission.story5_p: You found, in your neighbourhood, a single eatery that prepares dishes meeting your restrictions. Grateful, you visit every day. But there must be others. Somewhere in the same city, restaurants you simply have not encountered yet. — The energy to search is whittled away, alongside the restriction itself.
mission.story6_p: The dining table carries many constraints and convictions. The vegan colleague. The halal friend. The gluten-free family member. The child with an egg allergy. — You wish to host them. But finding that single seat is harder than imagined. Hidden seasonings unwritten on the menu. Shared utensils in the kitchen. Animal-based broths concealed within. Today's dining table lacks the means to discern "the establishments that take this seriously." The wish to host, before the constraint, quietly fades.
mission.story7_lead: A chef who has opened a bistro in Nagoya.
mission.story7_p: And now — let us shift our perspective from the diner to the maker. The chef who has just opened a bistro in Nagoya thinks: "I want to serve, in my restaurant, the dishes most popular in Lyon right now. I want my Nagoya regulars to taste them." Yet even were he to fly to Lyon, the number of restaurants he could visit is limited. By the time he returns, the trend has already moved on. — On the maker's side too, there are nights one stands still before the border.
mission.story_coda: Restaurants have crossed borders. Yet dishes have not. — And the dishes that have not crossed continue, quietly, to make both the eaters and the makers give up. These mismatches — we wish to challenge them.
mission.approach_p1: Information about food is already abundant. What we confront is not the lack of information, but the lack of connection between information and people. And the lack of access to the dishes themselves — those that have not crossed borders.
mission.approach_p2: It is not enough simply to translate dish names into another language. It is not enough to lay out menus in text. What is needed is a system that, considering each person's background — their mother tongue, their food culture, their allergies and convictions, their health, the preferences of those they care for, tonight's budget — places before them the dish they ought to meet now. And for the makers, it must be a circuit through which the latest trends and wisdom from the world's tables can arrive.
mission.approach_p3: We will, through technology and artificial intelligence, build this scene-specific matching. To decipher menus while travelling. To find doors not yet opened in the city. To reach the establishment that serves the cup you long for. And to deliver the dishes from beyond the border, to this side of the border. — One scene at a time, one product at a time.
mission.first_h: Our first challenge: beyond the menu.
mission.first_p1: PAAN's first product is MENU-PAAN, a menu translation service.
mission.first_p2: The night you stood frozen before a menu at a restaurant abroad. The moment a visitor to Japan gave up before the calligraphic menu of an izakaya. — These quiet walls of "I cannot read this" we shall lift, with the power of AI and many languages. Not mere literal translation, but a translation that returns to the food culture of the user's mother tongue. Allergy alerts, and explanations of each dish steeped in cultural context, from a single photograph.
mission.first_p3: This is the first step of our larger vision. Among the many regrets of the dining table, we begin with the wall that travellers face. With the technology and philosophy cultivated here, we shall expand into the next product, and the one after that.
mission.beyond_p1: What we aim for is not a menu translation company. Through the act of dining, we envision a world where people meet people, and cultures meet cultures, one step deeper than before.
mission.beyond_p2: Just as the Michelin Guide once made restaurants known beyond borders. We, in this era, wish to deliver the dishes themselves beyond borders. The pairing of Kanazawa's local sake, into the hands of sommeliers around the world. To bind, more deeply, the table of the eater and the table of the maker.
mission.beyond_p3: The dishes we wish to eat. The drinks we long for. — We shall press onward, beyond the gourmet site, opening the path to dishes yet undiscovered. Believing, surely, that beyond it lies the path to a world yet undiscovered.

brand.title: Brands | PAAN Co., Ltd.
brand.description: Brands operated by PAAN Co., Ltd. Including MENU-PAAN, our multilingual menu translation service.
brand.menupaan_desc: A multilingual menu translation service for both international travellers and inbound visitors to Japan. From a single photograph, we deliver — in your mother tongue — culturally contextual dish explanations and automated alerts for allergies, religious requirements, and dietary restrictions. Beyond the dry literal translation of existing apps, we offer a translation rooted in the user's own food culture.
brand.menupaan_feature_langs_value: Japanese / English / Chinese (Simplified & Traditional) / Korean / Spanish / French / German / Italian / Vietnamese / Indonesian / Thai
brand.menupaan_feature_func_value: Automated menu OCR, culturally contextual dish explanations, automated alerts for allergies, religion, and dietary restrictions, pairing recommendations, ordering support
brand.menupaan_feature_form_value: Web (PWA, all devices) / iOS app (rolling out)
brand.others_desc: To reach the cup you long for, by means other than chance. To meet the dish that pairs best with the local sake. To welcome, without hesitation, the table marked by dietary restriction or conviction. And to access dishes from beyond the border, from this side of the border. — The PAAN Series, realising one by one through products the scenes set forth in our mission, shall unfold in turn.

company.title: Company | PAAN Co., Ltd.
company.description: Company information and officers of PAAN Co., Ltd.
company.biz_1_main: Planning, development, and operation of MENU-PAAN
company.biz_1_sub: 12-language SaaS for international travellers and inbound visitors to Japan
company.biz_2_sub: Stock management, order management, multilingual menus, and other operational support systems
company.biz_3_sub: SaaS selection and implementation guidance, operational design, consultation on digitalisation
company.biz_4_strong: Research and development in food × AI
company.biz_4_sub: Research into dish understanding, culturally contextual translation, and scene-specific matching using artificial intelligence
company.founder_inui_bio: Graduated from Keio Senior High School in 2015. After completing his Bachelor of Laws (Politics) at Keio University in 2019, he joined the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Japan as a career-track officer. He served at headquarters covering Korea and the Philippines, and subsequently at the Embassy of Japan in the United Kingdom as Attaché and Second Secretary, engaged in external economic and diplomatic affairs. In June 2025, he left the Ministry and assumed the role of Managing Director at Kushinobou Co., Ltd., a kushikatsu specialist established in 1950. Drawing on his experience as both diplomat and restaurateur, he conceived MENU-PAAN. In May 2026, he co-founded the company. Born July 1996.
company.founder_yui_bio: Graduated from Keio Senior High School in 2015. While studying at Keio University Faculty of Law, he passed the preliminary bar examination in 2018 and the bar examination the following year. Upon his admission to the bar in 2021 (Daini Tokyo Bar Association), he joined ZeLo Law Office, where he specialises in startup finance, public affairs, and general corporate matters, providing legal advisory rooted in both public and private sector knowledge. After serving in administrative practice at the Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry's Innovation and Environment Bureau (Innovation Promotion Division), he is currently with Fugen Law Office. In May 2026, he co-founded MENU-PAAN. Born July 1996.

contact.title: Contact | PAAN Co., Ltd.
contact.description: Contact PAAN Co., Ltd. for partnerships, press, careers, and more.
contact.lead: For business partnerships, press enquiries, careers, and other matters, please reach out via the email address below. We will respond after reviewing your enquiry.
contact.email_note: We aim to respond within three business days (Mon-Fri, excluding public holidays). For urgent matters, or if you do not receive a reply within a reasonable time, we kindly ask you to send your message again.
contact.inquiry_1_body: We welcome partnerships with restaurants, travel and tourism providers, local governments, media, and companies in the translation and AI sectors.
contact.inquiry_2_body: For press, articles, and speaking engagements, please include your outlet, scope, and proposed dates.
contact.inquiry_3_body: We are recruiting engineers, designers, business development, and operations colleagues. Please include a brief background with your enquiry.
contact.inquiry_4_body: For MENU-PAAN service usage and bug reports, please use the contact form on menupaan.com.
